titleEn: Do agricultural spray drones actually pay off?
titleZh: 农业喷雾无人机真的划算吗？
summaryZh: 密苏里大学的研究揭示了农场主使用农业喷雾无人机的盈亏平衡点及隐藏成本。研究分析了购买成本、维护、电池寿命、飞行效率等因素，为种植业者提供了实用的投资决策参考。虽然该研究主要针对大田作物，但其成本效益分析方法对智慧畜牧中的无人机应用（如牧场监测、精准投喂）也具有借鉴意义。
featuredReason: 该研究为智慧畜牧行业人士提供了无人机成本效益分析的实用框架，尤其对牧场监测、精准投喂等场景的投资决策具有参考价值。
contentFull: Farming EquipmentTechnologyNewsDo agricultural spray drones actually pay off?Drones, Asked and Answered: University of Missouri research reveals breakeven point and hidden costs of drone ownership for farmers.Andy Castillo,Editor of Farm Equipment and Machinery,Farm ProgressJune 29, 2026schedule4 Min ReadSpray drones can cover 120 to 150 acres per day in optimal conditions, but achieving breakeven requires flying about 1,000 acres annually for eight years. guteksk7/Getty Images Agricultural spray drones’ effectiveness is well established. But what about their economic payback?“As any good economist would do, my answer is, ‘It depends,’” said Drew Kientzy, an agriculture business specialist at the University of Missouri Extension. “Drones are a very useful and unique tool.”But while useful, operating costs vary widely based on intensity of use, acreage covered, repair and maintenance, and fuel and lubrication, making it difficult sometimes to quantify payback. According to Gurbir Singh, soil science specialist at University of Missouri Extension, their definitive value, regardless of financial gain, comes through risk mitigation.“Timely management in ag is the key for success,” said Singh about spray drones’ ability to operate when fields are saturated. “This year is a perfect example. Our planting squeezed from six weeks or seven weeks to just two weeks because it was too wet throughout Missouri.”Related:Can irrigation automation be affordable? One former farmer says yesSingh highlighted waterhemp management in soybean fields as an example. Postemergence herbicide application can effectively control 2-inch weeds. But if there’s too much rain, they might grow to 6 inches or 1 foot before ground rigs are able to spray them. The herbicide loses its efficacy, and the crop must compete for sunlight and nutrients throughout the growing season. Without that timely spraying, Singh estimates potential yield losses of 40% to 70%. “The repercussions are huge,” he said. “Either I wait until next week for the ground to dry and take my spray rig out, or I fly a drone and catch up.” Timely drone application during wet conditions can prevent yield losses of 20% to 50% by controlling weeds before they outgrow herbicide effectiveness. Pictured is a drone flying during the University of Missouri’s research project. (Gurbir Singh)Singh backs up his expertise with data. Over four years, researchers from the University of Missouri conducted an economic analysis of drone ownership using Agras T20 and T40 drones, purchased for about $30,000 each. A truck and trailer with a generator cost an additional $100,000. Their breakeven came after flying about 1,000 acres annually for eight years, accounting for equipment depreciation and assuming personal operation. Ownership cost can be defrayed across acreage; drones become less expensive per acre the more they’re used.“On a good day with good batteries and everything, with the recommendations that we got from our data set, we could cover with a T40’s payload capacity of about 10 gallons, about 120 to 150 acres,” Singh said. That equates to roughly a solid week of spraying in good weather.Fast depreciationUnlike larger farm machinery, drones depreciate quickly. “The general cost structure of a drone has a pretty quick depreciable life,” Singh said. “While they might last five to seven years of fairly heavy use with good care and maintenance, typically, they’re going to become obsolete technologically more than likely within two, three, four years at most.”Related:Made for only a year, Tillsoil 18-30 a rare breedSingh began his drone economics research in 2021 using a T20 drone. “By the time we were ready to publish everything, the drone was obsolete. Then we bought a T40 drone and evaluated it for another two years. By the time we put it out, now that was obsolete as well,” he said, noting replacement parts can still be accessed even if the manufacturer has stopped supporting it. “If I’m a farmer and I invest in green or red equipment, I want that company to maintain the equipment at least for its life, for five, 10 years,” Singh said. “With drones, that is not the case.”Drones depreciate quickly, becoming technologically obsolete within two to four years, unlike traditional farm equipment that lasts decades. (Fenton Roman/Getty Images)Evaluating drone payback strictly by acreage doesn’t account for the variables of individual farms. Most farmers, for example, probably already own a truck. Some might be able to make do with an inexpensive trailer, perhaps one that’s homemade. And while an older used drone won’t have the same capabilities as the latest model, it’s easier to cash-flow for smaller operations, or those that don’t have as many saturated low-lying areas.“It depends on how creative you want to be and what your appetite is for investing time into your operation versus investing capital,” Kientzy said. “It’s pretty easy to end up with a bigger investment tied up in your supporting rig than the actual drones themselves. While it’s important to consider the math behind the drones, being able to think through the logistics of your support truck and trailer, and have that kind of well thought out is an important factor.”Related:Farmall Regular recalls roots of modern farmingQuantifying costs and benefitsLed by Singh, the University of Missouri created a tool that can estimate the breakeven for spray drone investment based on a farm’s unique variables, including acreage and expenses. To see drones fly in person, attend the 2026 Farm Progress Show on Sept. 1-3 in Boone, Iowa. Find out more at FarmProgressShow.com. Read more about:DronesAsked and AnsweredAbout the AuthorAndy CastilloEditor of Farm Equipment and Machinery, Farm ProgressAndy Castillo started his career in journalism about a decade ago as a television news cameraperson and producer before transitioning to a regional newspaper covering western Massachusetts, where he wrote about local farming. Between military deployments with the Air Force and covering the news, he earned an MFA in creative nonfiction writing from Bay Path University, building on the English degree he earned from the University of Massachusetts Amherst. In his role at Farm Progress, Castillo covers agricultural technology, new machinery and the constantly evolving farm equipment marketplace.Castillo splits his time between the open road and western Massachusetts with his wife, Brianna, a travel nurse who specializes in pediatric oncology, and their rescue pup, Rio. When not attending farm shows, Castillo enjoys playing music, snowboarding, writing, cooking and restoring their 1920 craftsman bungalow. translationZh:
密苏里大学的研究揭示了农场主使用农业喷雾无人机的盈亏平衡点及隐藏成本。

**研究背景**

随着精准农业技术的普及，越来越多的农场主开始考虑使用无人机进行农药喷洒。然而，这种设备是否真的能带来经济效益？密苏里大学的研究团队对这一问题进行了深入分析。

**关键发现**

研究显示，无人机的盈亏平衡点取决于多个因素，包括农场规模、作物类型、地形条件以及无人机的购买和维护成本。对于小型农场，无人机的投资回报周期可能较长，而大型农场则更容易实现盈利。

**隐藏成本**

除了购买成本，农场主还需要考虑电池更换、维修保养、软件升级以及操作培训等隐藏成本。这些费用可能占到总成本的30%以上，显著影响投资回报率。

**实践建议**

研究建议农场主在购买无人机前进行详细的成本效益分析，并考虑租赁或共享服务模式以降低初始投资。同时，选择适合当地作物和地形的无人机型号至关重要。
contentHtml: University of Missouri research reveals breakeven point and hidden costs of drone ownership for farmers.
